coredata.dc:title: Color-tunable and high-quantum-yield afterglow of carbon dots by covalent fixation
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wenhai
Li, Jian-An
Ma, Shushu
Chai, Zebin
Huang, Shan
Zhao, Yunpeng
Wang, Sujuan
Chen, Yang
Azad, Fahad
Chen, Hongyu
Yue, Dewu
Xu, Bingjia
Su, Shichen
coredata.dc:description: Because of the difficulties in populating and stabilizing the high energy triplet excited states, achieving violet organic afterglow at ambient conditions is still a formidable challenge. In this work, we propose an efficient strategy to activate violet and deep-blue afterglow of high energy triplet excited state by in situ embedding of carbon dots (CDs) into cyanuric acid crystals (CA) (named CDs@CA). The formation of covalent bonds in CDs@CA were found crucial in activating the afterglow with lifetime and phosphorescence quantum yields up to 1.80 s and 39%, respectively. Furthermore, the relationship between luminescence and copolymerization (covalent bonding) of CDs@CA were explored, which provides evidence for the regularity of multiple luminescent centers. A light-stimulated response of CDs@CA was found useful for applications in advanced anti-counterfeiting. These results provide a foundation for designing highly efficient violet and deep-blue organic afterglow materials with potential applications in the fields of bioimaging, anticounterfeiting, and optoelectronic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triplet exciton
Violet and deep-blue afterglow
Carbon dots
Matrices
Stimulated response materials

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of highly efficient violet/deep-blue afterglow materials
The mechanism of highly efficient afterglow of CDs@CA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of CDs@CA: (a) Reaction mechanism of CDs@CA for the preparation of highly efficient violet/deep-blue afterglow materials; (b) schematic of the closed setup in which reaction was conducted; (c) XRD patterns of CDs@CA and CA; (d) TEM image of CDs@CA.

Photophysical properties of doped CDs@CA: (a) Fluorescence excitation (Fluo. Exc.) (λem = 425 nm), Phosphorescence excitation (Phos. Exc.) (λem = 425 nm), Fluo. emission (λex = 345 nm), and Phos. emission (λex = 345 nm) spectra; (b) Time-resolved Phos. emission under 365 nm excitation without filter. Time-resolved Phos. emission under 365 nm excitation with (c) the filter-1 and (d) filter-2; (e) Fluo. decay spectra and fitting curves of CDs@CA and CA under ambient condition; (f) Phos. decay spectra of CDs@CA and CA; (g) The phosphorescent photographs of CDs@CA when radiation of different wavelengths went off.
Characterization of CDs@CA: (a) High resolution XPS spectra of C-1s (b) N-1s, and (c) O-1s; (d) FT-IR spectra; (e) Abs. spectra; and (f) EPR spectra from UM0.02 to UM0.6 for CDs@CA and CA.

Efficient afterglow mechanism of CDs@CA: (a) Diagrams of calculated energy levels and the possible ISC channels for different polymer molecules in CDs and Fluo. and Phos. emission of CDs@CA under 330 nm excitation. The three molecules are called luminescent units, LU-1(left), LU-2 (middle), and LU-3 (right), respectively; (b) the whole system structure of CDs@CA; (c) High performance summary of afterglow in recent two years.

Universality of the high-performance design strategy: (a) Chemical structures of matrices and photographs of mGH, mGC, mGP, and mUB taken under daylight, 365 nm, 254 nm with UV light (UV on) and after removal of excitation (UV off) for several seconds; (b) Fluo. and Phos. emission spectra and (c) corresponded Phos. lifetimes of mGH, mGC, mGP, and mUB.
Light-stimulated response of CDs@CA afterglow display for anti-counterfeiting applications.

head.ce:title: Color-tunable and high-quantum-yield afterglow of carbon dots by covalent fixation

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Natural Sciences (SNS), National University of Sciences and Technology (NUST), Islamabad, 44000, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Information Technology Research Institute, Shenzhen Institute of Information Technology, Shenzhen, 518172, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Information Technology Research Institute, Shenzhen Institute of Information Technology, Shenzhen, 518172, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Because of the difficulties in populating and stabilizing the high energy triplet excited states, achieving violet organic afterglow at ambient conditions is still a formidable challenge. In this work, we propose an efficient strategy to activate violet and deep-blue afterglow of high energy triplet excited state by in situ embedding of carbon dots (CDs) into cyanuric acid crystals (CA) (named CDs@CA). The formation of covalent bonds in CDs@CA were found crucial in activating the afterglow with lifetime and phosphorescence quantum yields up to 1.80 s and 39%, respectively. Furthermore, the relationship between luminescence and copolymerization (covalent bonding) of CDs@CA were explored, which provides evidence for the regularity of multiple luminescent centers. A light-stimulated response of CDs@CA was found useful for applications in advanced anti-counterfeiting. These results provide a foundation for designing highly efficient violet and deep-blue organic afterglow materials with potential applications in the fields of bioimaging, anticounterfeiting, and optoelectronic devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Efficient utilization of triplet exciton has attracted extensive interest due its potential application in sensing, light-emitting diodes and photovoltaics etc. [1–5]. The modulation of slow decay triplet excitons due to intricate and multiple competitive decay channels causes great difficulty in enhancing the luminescence for afterglow [6–9]. In order to enhance afterglow at ambient conditions, the challenges of triplet exciton activation to suppress nonradiative transitions (k nr) need to be addressed. A series of feasible structural design strategies have been proposed to obtain high-efficiency afterglow, including host–guest interactions [10–13] and El-Sayed rule [14]. However, Stokes shift associated with the structural relaxation at singlet excited state (S1) state as well as the vibronic coupling between S1 state and ground state (S0) restrict exciplex formation strategies in host-guest system for realization of violet or deep-blue organic afterglow [15–18]. The small ΔE ST between S1 and T1 leads to fast rate constants of intersystem crossing (ISC) and reverse intersystem crossing (RISC) (k isc and k risc, respectively) which compete with non-radiative channels (k nr) [17,19,20]. The materials with delayed fluorescence have small ΔE ST and associated with RISC of spin-forbidden triplets account 75% among electrically-generated excitons [1]. Efficient violet and deep-blue organic afterglow materials were realized by host and activator system [21–23], however, realization of efficient violet and deep-blue organic afterglow with long lifetime (>1.0 s) and high phosphorescence quantum yield (>30%) remains difficult.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new photoluminescent (PL) material, have attracted much interest in recent years due to their excellent photophysical properties, chemical stability, biocompatibility, and facile preparation [24–28]. CDs-based afterglow materials with multiple afterglow color including violet and deep-blue organic have also recently been developed, in which multi-colors CDs are embedded into a specific matrix [29]. These matrices can effectively fix the emitting material and prevent quenching of triplet excitons of CDs. More impressively, CDs with polymer-like structure benefit from crosslink-enhanced emission (CEE) effect, which can greatly suppress nonradiative transitions and generate afterglow [30–32]. CEE effect, a novel concept, conceived by Yang and co-authors, sheds light on the emission mechanism of CDs [32]. The cross-linking during the formation of CDs can also lead to a narrower ΔE ST of the synthesized CDs, which can promote ISC and facilitate spin-orbit coupling in the matrices [33]. Further studies suggest that formation of covalent bonds between matrices and CDs plays pivotal roles in activating afterglow and preventing its quenching from oxygen and water [34,35]. Despite these encouraging achievements, the limited selectivity of matrices put restrictions on CDs afterglow; therefore, the general rules for effective afterglow of host matrices need to be developed.

In this work, a general strategy by the heat treatment of m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and urea was proposed to elongate lifetime of violet and deep-blue organic afterglow and boost phosphorescence (Phos.) quantum yields (QY) of afterglow. The presence of covalent and hydrogen bonding in CDs@CA were found to greatly improve afterglow such that phosphorescence lifetimes were improved up to 1.80 s and the phosphorescence quantum yield to 39.3%. Furthermore, the formation of –CO groups in CDs significantly facilitates the intersystem crossing and increases violet/deep-blue Phos. QY. CDs-based materials display various violet and deep-blue afterglow by further modifying and replacing the matrices which greatly facilitates the universal design of high-performance organic afterglow materials. Furthermore, the relationships between luminescence and copolymerization in CDs@CA were explored and evidence for the regularity of multiple luminescent centers is provided. Based on the excellent optical property, the light-stimulated response of CDs@CA is proposed for applications in advanced anti-counterfeiting. This study provides an insight for designing highly efficient long-lifetime afterglow materials and along with detailed explanation of emission profiles in CDs.

Cyanuric acid, biuret, guanidine hydrochloride, guanidine carbonate, guanidine phosphate, and boric acid were purchased from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd., Whereas, urea and m-phenylenediamine (>99.5%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. Filter-1, Optical band pass filter (350–400 nm), was purchased by Shenzhen Infrared Laser Technology Co., Ltd. The filter-2 comes from Ultraviolet Analyzer of the filter.

The TEM images were obtained by JEM-2100HR. XPS measurements were performed using AXIS SUPRA spectrometer. ERP spectra were collected by Bruker A300. FT-IR measurements were taken using Nicolet 6700. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by BRUKER D8 ADVANCE. The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were recorded by FLS980, respectively. Phosphorescence lifetimes and fluorescence quantum yield were characterized by FLS980. Raman measurements were performed using in Via spectrometer. Time resolved Phos. emission was performed using Ocean Optics spectrophotometer (QE65 Pro). UV–visible absorption spectra were measured by a LAMBDA950 instrument. Photocurrents were measured by the electrochemical workstation (CHI660E).

CA was synthesized by mixing 0.6 g urea and 600 μl H2O into a 25 mL Teflon. It was then kept for heating at 300 °C for 3 h, keeping the environment closed. The autoclave was then allowed to cool down to room temperature, naturally. The powder was then washed three times with ethanol and water (1:1) to remove impurities. 0.002 g of m-phenylenediamine was dispersed into 20 ml of water (0.1 mg/ml). CDs@CA-0.01 wt% (CDs@CA) was synthesized by mixing m-phenylenediamine (0.1 mg/ml) in the above solution and following the same method. Synthesis of other samples are in the supporting literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of highly efficient violet/deep-blue afterglow materials
ce:section.ce:para: CDs@CA was fabricated with low amounts of MPD dispersed into urea by conventional solid-phase method in a Teflon at 300 °C under closed environment (Fig. 1a and b and S1, Supporting Information). As a contrast, undoped MPD under the same conditions was synthesized (called CA). The possible reaction routes of urea and MPD are shown in Fig. S2 [35,36]. According to Figs. S3–S4 and Tables S1-S6, the samples with lower ratios of CDs@CA with reaction temperature of 300 °C and time 3 h (with MPD doping of 0.01 wt%) exhibited better optical performances. To conveniently examine structural information, the sample of 0.01 wt% doped MPD was discussed in this study. The diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern (Fig. 1 c) at 28.1° and 29.8° can be assigned to cyanurate and cyanuric acid, respectively [37–40]. Therefore, the conversion from urea to cyanuric acid was confirmed by XRD analysis. The slight variation in the X-ray diffraction peaks of CDs@CA as compared to CA, might be due to stacking and the crystallization orientation of the CA molecules due to CDs embedding in them [36]. The successful growth of CDs into CA crystals was clearly confirmed by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis (Fig. 1d). No lattice structure was observed in HR-TEM analysis, which confirms amorphous nature of CDs resulting from covalently crosslinked frameworks [31,41].

The optimum Fluo. excitation and Phos. excitation spectra of CDs@CA are located at 345 nm, while the corresponding Fluo. and Phos. spectra are located at ∼425 nm (Fig. 2 a). CDs@CA displayed relatively high Phos. QY (34.1%) (Fig. S3 and Table S1) along with multiple peaks under different excitations. These emissions with multiple peaks are similar to free CDs, indicating exhibition of multiple luminescent centers (Fig. S5) [36,42–44]. Moreover, this result also suggests that CDs were successfully embedded in CA crystals and are likely responsible for the observed afterglow from CDs@CA. The Fluo. and Phos. spectra under different excitations, exhibit tiny and broad peaks around 400 nm, indicating violet delayed Fluo. spectra. To further investigate the afterglow of the CDs@CA, time-resolved afterglow spectra was observed. The afterglow was gradually redshifted from ∼425 to 470 nm, implying coexistence of delayed Fluo. and Phos. emission as time passed from 0 to 8 s (Fig. 2b) [23,45–48]. Notably, Fluo. emission displays more than one peak at ∼365 and ∼425 nm (Fig. S5). CDs@CA exhibits violet emission (<400 nm) with different filters due to delayed Fluo. spectra (Fig. 2c and d). Moreover, CDs@CA still exhibits a Phos. lifetime of 0.45 s with a Phos. emission of 385 nm under the excitation of 302 nm, indicating presence of violet afterglow (Fig. 2f and S6). The Fluo. emission spectra shown in Fig. 2e exhibits typical triple-exponential decay behavior (τ1 is one of the shortest). The prepared CDs@CA possesses a longer decay (τ1 = 1930 ps), indicating that the decay of CDs@CA could be further slacked. In addition, the samples with higher doping concentration of MPD in CA also exhibit slower decay channels (Fig. S3e and Table S2). These samples exhibited typical triple-exponential decay with the maximum average Phos. lifetime of CDs@CA (1.8 s), which is higher than that of CA (Fig. 2f and Table S3). These results suggest that the samples of CDs@CA show multiple luminescent centers. Hence, there is an afterglow of as-prepared samples including powders and dispersed liquid from violet to deep blue color range after switching off 302 and 365 nm light (Fig. 2g).

In general, a smaller ΔE
ST value benefits efficient ISC process to promote Phos. emission. Therefore, tailoring energy gap ΔE
ST value to a suitable range is critical for simultaneously producing delayed Fluo. and Phos. emission. RISC determines lifetime of TADF, so its decay rate constant (k
RISC ) can be used to estimate ΔE
ST of a material according to Eq. (1):
(1)
k
R I S C
= A ⋅ exp
(
−
Δ E
S T
/
k B
T
)
where A is a constant, k
B stands for the Boltzmann's constant and T is the absolute temperature in Kelvin [6,9,49]. For CDs@CA, it was found (ΔE
ST < 0.3 eV) to be smaller than CA (ΔE
ST > 0.3 eV). Hence, the reduction of ΔE ST induces the high phosphorescent efficiency (Table S6, Supporting Information).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of highly efficient afterglow of CDs@CA
ce:section.ce:para: To further clarify origin of the afterglow, XPS spectra of CA and CDs@CA were collected and analyzed. The XPS spectra demonstrate that CA and different doped concentration of CDs@CA are mainly composed of C, N, and O elements (Fig. S7e and Tables S4 and S5). C-1s XPS spectra (Fig. 3 a) exhibit three binding energies at 284.6, 286 and 288.8 eV, indicating the presence of CC, C–N and N–CO, respectively. With the gradually increase of doping concentrate of MPD, it is plausible to suggest that covalent bonding occurred during in-situ formation of CDs and CA due to the increased content of CC and C–N (Fig. S7g) [35,36,50]. The fitted spectra for N-1s shows three peaks at 398.4 eV 399.6 eV, and 400.6 eV, which can be assigned to N–CN, (C)3–N, and N–H bonds, respectively (Fig. 3b). The copolymerization because of covalent bonding in CDs@CA was confirmed by decreased content of N–H. The increased content of N–H from CDs@CA-0.01 wt% to CDs@CA-0.1 wt% means that more hydrogen bond connections were formed in CDs@CA crystals, giving rise to the change of crystal stack from cyanic acid to cyanurate (Fig. S7h) [37,40,51]. The relationship between change of the crystal stack and Phos. QY is further explained in Fig. S9, Supporting Information. The O-1s XPS spectra indicate presence of CO (531.4 eV), and C–OH (532.3 eV) (Fig. 3c) [31,49,52]. The increased content of N–CN and CO in O-1s and N-1s XPS spectra of CDs@CA, corresponds to the more Phos. QY (Fig. S3a). Abs. spectra of CA and CDs@CA show typical characteristic absorption peaks at 215 nm, and 345 nm, which can be attributed to π-π* transitions of CC and n-π* transitions of CO/C–N, respectively (Fig. 3d). The Fluo. Exc. and Phos. Exc. spectra of CDs@CA overlap with the absorption band, indicating that the photoluminescence probably occurred from CO and C–N (including N–CN and CC–N) moieties [7,53]. With continuous increase of MPD doping, n-π* content increases and π-π* decreases, which is different from CDs@CA. It might be due to formation of non-radiation defects introduced by the doping. EPR spectroscopy was used to confirm the existence of defects. The trace doping of MPD (0.01 wt %) caused significant increase in g-factor of CDs@CA as compared to CA. The g-factor was found to be 2.008 (2.003) for CDs@CA and which corresponds to enhancement of Phos. lifetime (Fig. 3e). These results indicate that trace number of defects can effectively improve the Phos. lifetime and QY while maintaining relatively unbroken crystal structure. The EPR intensity of the samples with increased concentration of MPD was further enhanced, which is compatible with increased C–N content as observed from the XPS and Abs. spectra (Figs. S7a–b). Meanwhile, CDs@CA present a transition from crystal to amorphous structure with increased doping concentration of MPD according to Abs. spectra, Raman spectra and XRD patterns (Figs. S7c–e), resulting in relatively low Phos. QY and lifetime [54,55]. The possible relationship between configuration of n-π* and Phos. lifetime was presented in the supporting documents (Fig. S8). FT-IR spectra of CDs@CA showed stronger characteristic peaks at 785 cm−1 and 1727 cm−1, which can be attributed to N–CN and –CO stretching vibrations, respectively (Fig. 3f). In FT-IR spectra, several peaks between 1100 cm−1 to 1700 cm−1 dominate characteristic stretching modes of C–N heterocycles, further suggesting the formation of covalent bond between CDs and CA [36,37]. Therefore, it is plausible to suggest that the appropriate content of CO and C–N can benefit Phos. lifetime and QY. In addition, similar performance was observed for CDs@CA in different reaction time and urea substitutes (Figs. S9–S11). For comparison, urea was replaced by cyanuric acid (mCA) to synthesize CDs-based afterglow materials under the same reaction conditions with CDs@CA (Fig. S10). It was observed that no significant improvement of phosphorescence quantum yield was seen, whereas phosphorescence lifetime didn't decrease. This indicates that hydrogen bonding plays an important role in phosphorescence lifetime.

The band gaps of all samples were determined using ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectra and XPS spectra. From the calculated band gaps values and measured valence band energy of all samples, the band positions were outlined in Fig. S12. The XRD and EPR results show the presence of CDs@CA with main structure of CA, which means CDs@CA occupies only a small part of CA. Therefore, it is evident that CDs@CA is composed of CDs@CA and CA heterojunction. Furthermore, the radiation in multi-channels is potentially due to charge transfer (CT) process (Figs. S13 and S14, and Table S6) [10,11,56,57].

Furthermore, formation of –CO groups in CDs@CA system facilitates ISC and increases Phos. QY. In CDs with self‐crosslinking of precursor, the CEE effect should be taken into account. In CEE photoluminescence (PL) mechanism, the electron-rich heteroatom functional groups defined as sub-luminophore are immobilized by cross-linking polymers to restrict the vibration and rotation of sub-fluorophores (-CO). This leads to increase of radiative transition and decrease of the non-radiative transition, thereby enhancing sub-fluorophores PL of CDs@CA [58]. During synthesis, non-conjugated polymer dots can be combined by the CEE effect (covalent or hydrogen bonding). The effect of covalent bonding in CDs is mainly discussed since covalent bonds play an effective role in the property of phosphorescence according to XPS results. Theoretical calculation based on density functional theory (DFT) was carried out. As small molecules (MPD) and polymer precursors (urea) can be copolymerized by covalent cross-linking in different degrees and the products could be embedded into the interior or at surface of CDs (Fig. S2), therefore, three molecules with different degrees of copolymerization were designed and shown in Fig. 4 a. Our results suggested that the luminescent units have phosphorescence wavelength from 380 nm to 560 nm, which agrees well with the experimental measurement. Multiple luminescent centers for CDs may originate from these molecules with different degrees of polymerization (Fig. 4b). In addition, we analyzed the characters of the excited states using the nature transition orbitals (NTOs) for three molecules [59]. It is well known that spatial separation of hole and electron NTOs can lead to charge transfer characters, thus resulting in small ΔE
ST . The degree of spatial separation of the hole NTOs and electron NTOs can be qualitatively judged by their corresponding electron cloud density distributions around the whole molecular structure. Clearly, as suggested in Fig. S15, from HOMO to LOMO, the hole NTOs and electron NTOs distributions of LU-1 show more distinct spatial separation than that of LU-2 and LU-3, suggesting that LU-1 has charge transfer characters, which leads to a small ΔE
ST (ΔE
ST = 0.1 eV) [60]. Furthermore, the low degree of copolymerization (LU-1) with considerable large SOC constants (ξ, 2.27 cm−1, S1 → T1) has benefited in enhancing ISC [6]. The strong SOC is beneficial for the production of triplet excitons in CDs@CA. With further increase of copolymerization (ξ, 1.09 cm−1, LU-2 and 1.03 cm−1, LU-3 for S1 → T1), the ISC process gets weakened by increased ΔE
ST due to the poor property of phosphorescence at longer reaction times and higher doping concentration (Figs. S3 and S9) [61,62]. Notably, the realization of violet Phos. emission is related to the degree of low polymerization of CDs@CA, even though the relationship between luminescence intensity at different wavelengths and different molecular contents may hardly be quantitatively analyzed.

Based on the above discussion, mechanism for activation of CD@CA composite's afterglow is proposed as follows. The covalent bonding, (due to increased content of C–N) between CDs and CA as result of in situ CDs embedding into CA matrices through simple solid phase reaction, provides effective fixation and rigidification effect to the triplet excitons of CDs. Furthermore, low polymerization for CDs with the appropriate content of –CO functional groups (CEE effect) with was found to facilitate ISC and increase the Phos. QY. The performance comparison between CD@CA and afterglow materials reported previously is shown in Fig. 4c. CD@CA composite has a considerable advantage compared to other afterglow materials. (Fig. 4c) [21,23,30,50,53,63–69].

According to the CEE effects, the CO functional group with lone pair electrons effectively improves the phosphorescence performance [33]. To verify the universality of this strategy, it is important to design high-performance afterglow materials using precursor with functional groups containing unpaired electrons (PO, CO, BO, and CN) and other functional groups that can be easily copolymerized (-N-H), such as guanidine salt and borate. We used three widely studied precursors: guanidine hydrochloride, guanidine carbonate, and guanidine phosphate, as the matrices (Fig. 5 a) for constructing corresponding composites of mGH, mGC, and mGP. Fig. 5b shows Phos. and Fluo. spectra under optimal excitation. The results showed huge increase in lifetime up to 1.64 s and Phos. QY of 17.7% with Phos. emission at 425 nm for deep-blue afterglow (Fig. 5b and c and S16). In addition, boric acid in CDs@CA system (called mUB) can also effectively change the Phos. emission [21,70,71]. The presence of boron nitride and boron oxide in mUB is responsible for higher energy level delayed Fluo. emission whereas boron nitride is critical for violet phosphorescence (Fig. 5a and b, and S17) and corresponding long phosphorescence lifetime (Fig. 5c). This is the first report of violet phosphorescence (<400 nm) with a long lifetime (>1 s) based on organic afterglow materials.

Considering the afterglow features, these samples can be used for advanced applications in anti-counterfeiting (Fig. 6 ). An anti-counterfeiting system was designed and fabricated employing phosphor of CDs@CA-0.01 wt%, −0.1 wt%, −0.5 wt%, and −1.0 wt% to write“SCNU” (South China Normal University). When the pattern of “SCNU” was irradiated at 302 nm, it displayed blue Fluo. emission. When the light was turned off, the pattern then showed violet and deep blue Phos. emission. When switching the light off after irradiating the phosphor (SCNU) at 365 nm, it exhibited blue phosphorescence emission.

In summary, a general strategy was proposed to develop, elongate lifetime and boost Phos. QY of violet/deep-blue organic afterglow by the heat treatment of MPD and urea. A series of samples of CDs@CA were synthesized and enhanced Fluo. and Phos. QY was achieved, due to covalent bonding between CDs and CA via C–N. The Phos. lifetimes were improved up to 1.80 s and the Phos. QY up to 39.3%. Furthermore, the appropriate content of –CO functional group in CDs was found to facilitate the intersystem crossing and increase the phosphorescence quantum yield. Furthermore, the relationship between violet/deep-blue afterglow and copolymerization in CDs@CA were explored to provide evidence for the regularity of multiple luminescent centers. Furthermore, it was found that multi-component doping of matrices can effectively realize long afterglow. The violet Phos. emission of CDs@CA was found useful in designing ultraviolet phosphorescence by further modifying dopants and matrix. This study expands the selectivity of afterglow materials and provides strategies for promoting high energy triplet exciton generation. These results provide a foundation for designing highly-efficient violet/deep-blue organic afterglow materials that greatly extends their potential applications in the fields of bioimaging, anticounterfeiting, and optoelectronic devices.

Wenhai Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Formal Analysis, Writing - Original Draft. Jian-An Li: Visualization, Investigation. Shushu Ma, Zebin Chai, Shan Huang, Yunpeng Zhao, Sujuan Wang, Yang Chen, Data Curation, Writing - Original Draft, Software, Validation. Fahad Azad, Writing - Review & Editing. Hongyu Chen, Dewu Yue, Bingjia Xu, Shichen Su, Writing - Review & Editing, Supervision. Bingjia Xu, Shichen Su: Conceptualization, Funding Acquisition, Resources, Supervision, Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11974122); Rural Science and Technology Commissioner Project (Grant No. KTP20210340); The Scientific and Technological Plan of Guangdong Province, China (Nos. 2019B090905005, 2020A1414010252, and 2022A0505050067) and The Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province of China (2019A1515010550). The authors would also like to sincerely thank Zhong Zhang at SCNU Qingyuan Institute of Science and Technology Innovation Co., Ltd for his assistance in applications in anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2022.119399.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient organic light-emitting diodes from delayed fluorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Uoyama, K. Goushi, K. Shizu, H. Nomura, C. Adachi, Highly efficient organic light-emitting diodes from delayed fluorescence, Nature 492 (2012) 234-238.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrafast long-range charge separation in organic semiconductor photovoltaic diodes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The role of spin in the kinetic control of recombination in organic photovoltaics
